design_philosophy.core_loop: Zone land for residential/commercial/industrial use, build infrastructure (power grid, transportation), adjust tax rates, monitor population needs and city metrics, respond to emergent problems and disasters
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Balancing economic growth, population satisfaction, environmental management, and infrastructure maintenance without explicit win/loss conditions in sandbox mode
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Become an urban planner and mayor; design, build, and manage a thriving city from inception through growth and crisis
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Rejects arcade/action gameplay entirely in favor of systems-driven emergent play. Emphasizes non-violent creative problem-solving and economic/political simulation. Demonstrates that games can be educational and addictive without combat or scores. Players must understand interconnected systems (zoning effects traffic, pollution affects housing grades, power distribution affects development patterns)

difficulty.checkpointing: Sandbox mode: no checkpoints, persistent city management. Scenario mode: time-limited challenges with win/loss conditions based on player performance
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: High agency—players can adjust tax rates, disaster frequency (in some versions), and playstyle. Sandbox mode removes pressure entirely. Scenario mode imposes external goals and time constraints

progression.upgrade_system: Infrastructure unlocks (roads, power lines, transit systems). Building types unlock as city infrastructure and population grow. Rewards (mayor's mansion, casino) unlock in SNES version upon scenario completion
progression.unlock_mechanism: Population growth and infrastructure development trigger availability of more advanced building types and structures
replayability_hooks: Multiple named scenarios with distinct cities and challenges
Endless sandbox mode with no defined win state
Ability to tackle scenarios with different strategies
Disaster events create unpredictable situations requiring adaptation

world_building_techniques: Scenario introductions establish context (real or fictional cities in crisis situations)
Environmental feedback: citizen satisfaction via density/infrastructure visualization, crime/pollution visible in map data
Implied narrative through city emergent behavior and player decisions
Disaster events create dramatic moments (monster attacks, tornadoes, earthquakes)

faction_indicators: Residential vs. industrial vs. commercial sector balance reflects player priorities
Tax policy signals economic philosophy (high tax = community-focused, low tax = business-friendly)
Investment in transit, parks, and public services vs. industry development
danger_signals: Unchecked industrial zones create pollution and crime
Neglecting power grid infrastructure causes widespread outages
Poor traffic planning creates congestion that stifles commerce
environmental_storytelling: Dense residential areas next to power plants visualize negative urban planning consequences
Crime rates cluster in under-resourced neighborhoods
Traffic congestion patterns reveal infrastructure inadequacy
Disaster aftermath shows vulnerability of poorly-planned cities
Visual city density and development patterns communicate player's urban design philosophy
